pattern: The robot only ever walks the outer rectangle boundary, so its state after any total number of steps is a pure function of that running total modulo the boundary's perimeter, with one special case: landing exactly on a whole multiple of the perimeter means it just arrived back at the origin heading in the final segment's direction, not the starting one.
hints: Simulating one grid cell at a time per step() call works but a single call can request up to 1e5 steps; notice the robot's path only ever traces the four edges of the rectangle in a fixed repeating cycle, so you don't need to move cell by cell.

Keep a single running total of all steps ever requested. The rectangle's perimeter has length 2*(width - 1 + height - 1); the robot's position and facing after the running total's steps depend only on that total modulo the perimeter.

Split the perimeter into four segments (east along the bottom, north along the right edge, west along the top, south along the left edge) with known lengths (width-1, height-1, width-1, height-1) and figure out which segment the reduced step count falls in to get position and direction directly. One subtlety: if the running total is a positive exact multiple of the perimeter, treat it as landing at the END of the south segment (position (0,0), facing South), not the start of the east segment — the robot has just arrived there via the last segment and has not yet attempted (and been blocked into turning for) its next move.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: a run that stays on the bottom edge, then one that wraps corners twice
smallest possible grid (2x2); a full lap returns to the origin facing South
smallest grid, exactly two full laps (an exact multiple greater than one perimeter) still lands facing South at the origin
one step short of a full lap faces West just before the final corner, and completing the lap exactly lands back at the origin facing South, not East
a very wide, short grid: many steps consumed entirely along the bottom and top edges before any north/south movement
many step() calls accumulate into a total larger than any single call's own max, and the running total (not any single call) determines the final state
num at the constraint's upper bound on a small grid forces many full laps

stress.description: random small grids with many step calls of modest size, querying position/direction after almost every call
stress.note: small grids with modest step sizes keep the brute-force oracle's unit-by-unit simulation fast while still wrapping many laps
